perturbation.original_text: 1. Definition . “Confidential Information” means, in respect to each Party: (a) any information in which such Party claims a proprietary and/or confidential interest; (b) all confidential matters of such Party including, without limitation, technical kn ow-how, trade secrets, technical data, new product strategy, new products or processes, analysis, compilations, concepts, technical processes, formulae, recipes, specifications, inventions, research projects, customer lists, pricing, pricing policies, operational methods, financial information, marketing information and other business affairs; (c) any information of a confidential nature concerning such Party’s customers, suppliers or employees; and (d) any information such Party has received from othe rs which they are obliged to treat as proprietary and/or confidential . The Confidential Information of either Party includes all of the above information with respect to any entity controlling, controlled by, or under common control with, such Party. Confidential Information shall not include information which: (a) is in the public domain prior to the date of its disclosure to Recipient by the Disclosing Party; (b) is known and can be shown to be known by Recipient prior to the date of its disclos ure to Recipient by Disclosing Party; (c) becomes part of the public domain by publication or otherwise, and is not the result of any unauthorized act or omission on the part of Recipient; (d) can be demonstrated to have been supplied to Recipient by a thi rd party who is under no obligation to the Disclosing Party to maintain such information in confidence; or (e) is independently developed by Recipient without the use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Definition . “Confidential Information” means information shared between the Parties during the term of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the detailed definition of 'Confidential Information,' and replacing it with a vague one, this creates an in-text contradiction. Later sections, such as Section 2 and Section 8, rely on the detailed definition of what constitutes Confidential Information, specifically the exceptions listed in (a) through (e) to determine the obligations of confidence and the survival period of those obligations. The new, extremely broad definition makes it impossible to determine what information is actually covered by the agreement, creating uncertainty around enforcement of all sections reffering to the original definition
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Obligation of Confidence . Each Party hereby ackno wledges and agrees that the Confidential Information constitutes valuable information, and the provisions of this Agreement are necessary to protect the secrecy and confidentiality thereof. FSMA FSMA Approved M utual Confidentiality 2013.05.24 2 (a) Recipient shall (i) maintain the Confidential Information i n strict confidence and take all reasonable steps to prevent its disclosure to third parties; (ii) use at least the same degree of care as Recipient uses in maintaining the secrecy of its own Confidential Information (but no less than a reasonable degree o f care); and (iii) prevent the removal of any proprietary, confidential or copyright notices placed on the Confidential Information. (b) Recipient may use the Confidential Information only in connection with the performance of the Agency Sales and Ma rketing Services with Distributor . Recipient shall not, at any time, make any use of the Confidential Information for any other purpose. (c) Recipient shall keep the Confidential Information confidential at all times and shall not disclose the Confid ential Information to any person including its employees except to its employees, representatives, advisors and agents who have a need to know such information in connection with assisting Recipient for the purposes set forth in paragraph 2(b) above and wh o are required to keep such information confidential. (d) Notwithstanding the foregoing in this Section 2, Recipient may disclose to Principals , on whose behalf it is performing Agency Sales and Marketing Services with Distributor , and who have a need to know the Confidential Information provided that: (i) Distributor has been notified of the identity of such Principals and has agreed to the disclosure of Confidentiality Agreement under this Section 2(d), (ii) only the necessary portions of Confidential Information required by such Principals to evaluate and utilize the Agency Sales and Marketing Services is disclosed, and (iii) Principal has a Confidentiality Agreement in place with Distributor or has a Confidentiality Agreement acceptable to Distributo r in place with Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to simply 'information shared between the Parties during the term of this agreement,' removing the original detailed definition and its exceptions. This creates a direct contradiction with Section 2, 'Obligation of Confidence,' which relies on the specific exceptions (a) through (e) in the original definition to delineate what information is protected and under what circumstances. The changed text's vague definition makes it impossible to apply Section 2's obligations as intended, leading to uncertainty and potential unenforceability. The explanation provided details how this removal creates the contradiction, and the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction fits the scenario.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Obligation of Confidence . Each Party hereby ackno wledges and agrees that the Confidential Information constitutes valuable information, and the provisions of this Agreement are necessary to protect the secrecy and confidentiality thereof. FSMA FSMA Approved M utual Confidentiality 2013.05.24 2 (a) Recipient shall (i) maintain the Confidential Information i n strict confidence and take all reasonable steps to prevent its disclosure to third parties; (ii) use at least the same degree of care as Recipient uses in maintaining the secrecy of its own Confidential Information (but no less than a reasonable degree o f care); and (iii) prevent the removal of any proprietary, confidential or copyright notices placed on the Confidential Information. (b) Recipient may use the Confidential Information only in connection with the performance of the Agency Sales and Ma rketing Services with Distributor . Recipient shall not, at any time, make any use of the Confidential Information for any other purpose. (c) Recipient shall keep the Confidential Information confidential at all times and shall not disclose the Confid ential Information to any person including its employees except to its employees, representatives, advisors and agents who have a need to know such information in connection with assisting Recipient for the purposes set forth in paragraph 2(b) above and wh o are required to keep such information confidential. (d) Notwithstanding the foregoing in this Section 2, Recipient may disclose to Principals , on whose behalf it is performing Agency Sales and Marketing Services with Distributor , and who have a need to know the Confidential Information provided that: (i) Distributor has been notified of the identity of such Principals and has agreed to the disclosure of Confidentiality Agreement under this Section 2(d), (ii) only the necessary portions of Confidential Information required by such Principals to evaluate and utilize the Agency Sales and Marketing Services is disclosed, and (iii) Principal has a Confidentiality Agreement in place with Distributor or has a Confidentiality Agreement acceptable to Distributo r in place with Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Obligation of Confidence . Each Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that the Confidential Information constitutes valuable information. (a) Recipient shall (i) maintain the Confidential Information in strict confidence and take all reasonable steps to prevent its disclosure to third parties; (ii) use at least the same degree of care as Recipient uses in maintaining the secrecy of its own Confidential Information (but no less than a reasonable degree of care); and (iii) prevent the removal of any proprietary, confidential or copyright notices placed on the Confidential Information. (b) Recipient may use the Confidential Information only in connection with the performance of the Agency Sales and Marketing Services with Distributor . Recipient shall not, at any time, make any use of the Confidential Information for any other purpose. (c) Recipient shall keep the Confidential Information confidential at all times and shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any person including its employees except to its employees, representatives, advisors and agents who have a need to know such information in connection with assisting Recipient for the purposes set forth in paragraph 2(b) above and who are required to keep such information confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting section 2(d) specifically, which speaks to the process on allowing recipents of confidential information to share said information with Principals if the distributor has been notified and agreed to the disclosure, This creates an in-text contradiction, by removing the possibility of sharing this data with principles, the agreement becomes more rigid and contradict the idea of mutual disclosure that is present in the documents name.
perturbation.contradicted_text: FSMA Approved Mutual Confidentiality 2013. 05.24 Distributor/ Sales and Marketing Agency Mutual Confidentiality and Non -Disclosure Agreement This Mutual Confidentiality and Non -Disclosure Agreement (this “Agreement” ) is entered into this ______ day of _____________ , 20__, by and between __________ _____ __________________, a _ __________entity, together with its operating subsidiaries and affiliates (“Distributor”) with its principal place of business at _____________________________________ __________ _, and _______ ___________________ _____________ , a _______ _____ entity, together with its operating subsidiaries and affiliates , (“Agency ”) with its principal place of business at _______________________________ ________ ________ (collectively the “Parties” ).
perturbation.justification: The original text included section 2(d) which allowed the recipient to disclose confidential information to Principals under certain conditions. The changed text omits this section entirely. The contradicted text, 'FSMA Approved Mutual Confidentiality,' establishes the document as a mutual confidentiality agreement. By removing the allowance for disclosure to Principals, the changed text creates a strong in-text contradiction. It eliminates an aspect of 'mutual' sharing as originally envisioned, thus creating a stricter confidentiality obligation. This directly conflicts with the stated intention of a 'Mutual Confidentiality' agreement and would cause confusion about the scope of permitted disclosures.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Return of Materials and Information . All Documents made available hereunder, including all copies, notes, summaries, and abstracts thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed upon completion of the Agen cy Sales and Marketing Services with Distributor or upon written request by the Disclosing Party, and Recipient shall certify in writing that it has complied with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Return of Materials and Information . All Documents made available hereunder, including all copies, notes, summaries, and abstracts thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party upon written request by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or destroyed upon completion of the Agency Sales and Marketing Services with Distributor' from Section 3, this creates an internal contradiction because the clause previously allowed for the destruction of materials upon completion, where as, with the new perturbation is requires them to be returned only upon written request.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Return of Materials and Information . All Documents made available hereunder, including all copies, notes, summaries, and abstracts thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party upon written request by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for the destruction of materials upon completion of the service. The changed text removes this option, requiring return only upon request. This creates a direct contradiction, as the contradicted text (which is also the changed text) no longer allows for the completion-based destruction that was previously permitted, creating a clear conflict with the prior agreement within the original text.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Survival . The foregoing obligations of all Parties shall continue for a period of two (2) years from the date of last disclosure of Confidential Information by either party, unless a specific portion of the Confidential FSMA FSMA Approved M utual Confidentiality 2013.05.24 3 Information disclosed becomes generally known to the public before the expiration of such two (2) year period through no fault of the Receiving Party . However, with respect to trade secrets, such obligations will survive for so long as such Confidential Information constitutes a trade secret under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Survival . The foregoing obligations of all Parties shall continue for a period of two (2) years from the date of last disclosure of Confidential Information by either party, unless a specific portion of the Confidential FSMA FSMA Approved M utual Confidentiality 2013.05.24 3 Information disclosed becomes generally known to the public before the expiration of such two (2) year period through no fault of the Receiving Party .
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'However, with respect to trade secrets, such obligations will survive for so long as such Confidential Information constitutes a trade secret under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.' from Section 8, it removes the trade secrets act, which means the section creates a contradiction. Now all Confidential Information is only being protected for 2 years, instead of trade secrets being protected longer.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. Survival . The foregoing obligations of all Parties shall continue for a period of two (2) years from the date of last disclosure of Confidential Information by either party, unless a specific portion of the Confidential FSMA FSMA Approved M utual Confidentiality 2013.05.24 3 Information disclosed becomes generally known to the public before the expiration of such two (2) year period through no fault of the Receiving Party .
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that obligations regarding trade secrets survive as long as the information constitutes a trade secret under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The changed text omits this clause, limiting all confidential information protection to two years. The contradicted text (which is the same as the changed text) now contradicts the implied protection of trade secrets beyond two years, which would be expected under the original version and the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The omission creates a direct conflict regarding the duration of protection for trade secrets, thus creating an in-text contradiction, adhering to the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.
